title: Dog Oozing Hot Spot on Hip: Triage and Treatment Guide
meta_desc: Learn about oozing hot spots on your dog's hip, the urgency for vet care, and how sharing a photo can help triage your pet's skin infection.
content_html:
What is an Oozing Hot Spot?

An oozing hot spot, medically known as acute moist dermatitis, is a rapidly progressing area of skin inflammation and bacterial infection. On a dog's hip, these lesions typically appear as red, moist, and painful patches where the hair may be missing or matted with discharge. They are often triggered by an initial irritation, such as a flea bite, allergy, or minor wound, which the dog then aggressively licks, bites, or scratches.

Is This a Veterinary Emergency?

The urgency level for an oozing hot spot is considered Medium. While it is rarely a life-threatening emergency, it is a very painful condition that can spread across the body within hours. Because of the high risk of secondary infection and the significant discomfort your dog is experiencing, you should seek veterinary care within 24 to 48 hours. If your dog is acting lethargic or has a fever, contact your vet sooner.

How a Photo Helps Triage

Taking a clear, well-lit photo of the hip lesion is an essential step in the triage process. A digital image allows your veterinarian to assess the severity of the inflammation and the extent of the discharge before you even arrive at the clinic. This visual data helps the medical team prioritize your pet's appointment and provides a baseline to monitor whether the spot is growing or healing once treatment begins.

faqs.question: Can I use human antibiotic cream on a hot spot?
faqs.answer: It is generally not recommended to use human ointments. Many contain ingredients that are toxic if licked, and thick creams can trap moisture, which often makes the bacterial infection worse.

faqs.question: What can I do at home before the vet appointment?
faqs.answer: The most important step is to prevent further trauma. Place an Elizabethan collar (cone) on your dog immediately to stop them from licking or biting the hip.

faqs.question: Will a hot spot go away on its own?
faqs.answer: Rarely. Because hot spots are itchy and painful, dogs continue to irritate the area, creating a cycle of self-trauma. Most require professional cleaning and a combination of antibiotics and anti-inflammatories to resolve.
